handoff_packages.pcp_summary:
Dear Colleague,

Sarah Williams is a 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus since age 14, admitted 2026-04-18 with severe diabetic ketoacidosis (pH 7.14, bicarbonate 8, anion gap 28) and discovered incidentally to be 7 weeks pregnant (serum hCG 28,450; she was unaware at admission). DKA resolved by hospital day 3 on IV insulin; she was transitioned to subcutaneous basal-bolus with glucose 112 at discharge. HbA1c on admission was 11.4%, reflecting severely uncontrolled disease entering a high-risk first trimester.

Discharge medications (reconciled per Pharmacist): insulin glargine 22 units SC QHS (increased from home 18 units for pregnancy insulin resistance), insulin aspart per pregnancy-adjusted sliding scale AC meals, prenatal vitamin daily, folic acid 4 mg PO daily, and a pregnancy-safe antihypertensive (agent per attending, pending lisinopril discontinuation). LISINOPRIL 10 mg AND ATORVASTATIN 20 mg WERE DISCONTINUED AT DISCHARGE due to absolute pregnancy contraindication — please do not resume during pregnancy; statin resumption deferred to postpartum.

Key labs (2026-04-22): Na 136, K 4.2, Cr 0.8, glucose 112, bicarbonate 23, anion gap 12, urine ketones trace. TSH 2.1 (upper limit of pregnancy first-trimester target); Free T4 pending — please recheck both at first visit. Prenatal ultrasound pending.

Active problems: T1DM severely uncontrolled in early pregnancy (HbA1c 11.4%, target <6.5%); essential hypertension on pregnancy-safe agent; obesity (BMI 31.8); teratogen exposure window documented; multiple active SDOH barriers (uninsured with Medicaid pending, food insecure, housing unstable, transportation-limited, no local caregiver).

LACE 13 (VERY_HIGH tier), driven by 4 prior DKA ED visits in 6 months consistent with access-driven insulin rationing, not non-adherence. Unresolved at discharge: Medicaid activation pending; insulin access secured via 30-day bridge only.

Recommended follow-up: MFM within 5-7 days (critical — confirmed before discharge), endocrinology within 2 weeks, you within 1 week. Please reinforce insulin access plan and screen for antenatal depression at every visit.

home_health_orders.visit_frequency: Consider RN home visit within 24-48 hours post-discharge pending insurance activation; weekly thereafter for first month if Medicaid activates, to verify insulin technique, glucose log review, and BP monitoring
home_health_orders.vital_monitoring: Self-monitored BP daily (provide cuff via charity care if possible); fingerstick glucose minimum 4x daily (fasting, pre-meal, bedtime) plus pre-cycling shift; urine ketones if glucose >200 or symptoms
escalation_thresholds: Call PCP/MFM: BG consistently >180 fasting or >200 post-meal on two readings; BG <70 requiring treatment (report pattern); urine ketones 1+ or greater; SBP >140 or DBP >90 on two readings 15 min apart; nausea/vomiting preventing oral intake >12h; unable to obtain insulin

Go to ED: BG >250 with any ketones OR BG >300 regardless; BG <54 or hypoglycemia with altered consciousness; unable to keep fluids/insulin down; SBP >160 or DBP >110; severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, facial swelling; vaginal bleeding or cramping; signs of DKA (Kussmaul breathing, fruity breath, confusion)

handoff_packages.patient_instructions:
Sarah, here is what you need to know before you go home.

Why you were in the hospital: Your blood sugar went very high and made you very sick. While we were treating you, we also learned you are 7 weeks pregnant.

Medicines that changed:
- STOP taking lisinopril and atorvastatin. These are NOT safe for the baby. Do not fill these at the pharmacy.
- Your long-acting insulin dose is now 22 units at bedtime (not 18). Pregnancy makes you need more insulin.
- You have a new blood pressure pill. Take it every day as written.
- Take your prenatal vitamin and folic acid 4 mg every day.

Danger signs — go to the ER right away:
- Blood sugar over 250 with ketones in urine, OR over 300 any time
- Blood sugar under 54, or you feel confused and sugar does not come back up
- Throwing up and cannot keep water or insulin down
- Top BP number over 160 or bottom over 110
- Bad headache, blurry vision, belly pain, or face swelling
- Any bleeding from the vagina

Call your doctor if: blood sugar stays high, you see ketones, or you cannot get your insulin.

Appointments: You MUST see the high-risk pregnancy doctor (MFM) within one week. Your team has the appointment. Keep your ride plan.

If you cannot afford insulin, CALL the number your social worker gave you BEFORE you run out.
handoff_packages.patient_instructions_note: Written at approximately 5th-grade reading level, 195 words.

meta.confidence_notes: All five agents provided complete, internally consistent outputs with strong convergence on the four CRITICAL blockers (two teratogens, insulin access, MFM referral). Physician explicitly marked discharge_ready: false. SDOH documentation is unusually detailed. Residual uncertainties: exact IPV/EPDS status (not yet screened), Medicaid activation timeline (external dependency), and real-time confirmation of prenatal ultrasound scheduling — none of which change the hold determination.

functional_mobility.endurance: Functionally high at baseline. Admission presentation included sinus tachycardia HR 128 and Kussmaul respirations — physiologic effects of DKA, not deconditioning. By Day 3, HR 84 and RR 16, indicating resolution of cardiorespiratory stress. No endurance limitation expected at discharge given 5-day LOS and robust pre-admission baseline.

Hypoglycemia risk while performing physically demanding work (bicycle courier) — particularly relevant given uncontrolled T1DM and sliding scale prandial insulin

7 weeks pregnant — early pregnancy dizziness/orthostatic changes possible, though not yet documented

rehab_recommendation.justification: Sarah Williams is a 42-year-old woman with Type 1 DM admitted for DKA who is functionally independent at a high level — her occupation as a bicycle courier confirms robust pre-morbid functional capacity. She has no functional mobility deficits, no deconditioning requiring structured rehabilitation, no gait dysfunction, no balance impairment, no neuromuscular or musculoskeletal sequelae from this admission, and no ADL limitations. Her 5-day hospitalization involved medical management of a metabolic crisis, not a functional insult. Inpatient PT evaluation was not clinically indicated and does not appear to have been performed, which is appropriate. This patient does not require IRF, SNF subacute rehab, home PT, or formal outpatient PT. She requires diabetes self-management education (DSME) and medical nutrition therapy — disciplines outside PT scope but critical for this case. No formal PT services are indicated at this time.

Housing instability — month-to-month lease, 6 weeks behind on rent. Risk of housing loss within the discharge window, which would eliminate a stable recovery environment

insurance_access_conflict.detail: Patient is uninsured. Medicaid application pending but not yet active. While PT services are not clinically indicated for this patient, the DME items flagged above (CGM, glucometer, strips, glucagon kit) are essential for safe home management of T1DM in pregnancy and fall under durable medical equipment coverage. Without insurance, CGM is cost-prohibitive ($200–400/month out of pocket). Glucagon kits cost $150–$350 out of pocket without coverage. These are not optional items — they are safety-critical for a patient with a recently adjusted insulin regimen, uncontrolled T1DM, active pregnancy, and occupational hypoglycemia risk.
insurance_access_conflict.clinical_need: CGM or glucometer with strips, glucagon rescue kit, and fingerstick supplies are medically necessary for safe discharge of a patient with T1DM on adjusted insulin therapy, active pregnancy, and occupational hypoglycemia exposure as a bicycle courier.
insurance_access_conflict.coverage_limit: Currently uninsured. Medicaid pending — not yet active. No DME coverage available at time of discharge.
resolution_options: Medicaid presumptive eligibility — expedite with social work; pregnancy may qualify patient for presumptive Medicaid activation before formal approval, which would cover DME
Hospital charity care provision of 30-day glucometer supplies and glucagon kit as part of discharge package

Abbott Libre Bridge Program — similar manufacturer assistance for FreeStyle Libre

Document medical necessity for all DME items in discharge summary to facilitate retroactive Medicaid billing once approved

Confirm patient has a designated emergency contact who can be reached by phone and who is educated on when to call 911. Consider whether a 24-hour post-discharge safety check-in call can be arranged by the care team or CHC.

flags.action: Formal bedside PT evaluation was not performed during this admission, which is clinically appropriate given the patient's functional presentation. This assessment is based on documented nursing ADL assessment, physician functional status notation, and patient-reported occupational history. Confidence is moderate rather than high as a result. No PT follow-up action required — flag is for documentation transparency only.

flags.flag: FOOD INSECURITY DIRECTLY UNDERMINES INSULIN REGIMEN SAFETY IN THE HOME ENVIRONMENT
flags.action: From a functional safety standpoint, the home environment cannot support a meal-time sliding scale insulin regimen if the patient cannot consistently access food. Irregular meals on prandial insulin creates predictable hypoglycemia risk in the home. PT scope does not extend to nutrition intervention, but this hazard is flagged to the team. Medical nutrition therapy referral and emergency food access (food pantry, WIC, SNAP enrollment) are essential discharge planning elements that directly affect home safety for this patient.

pt.confidence_note: No formal PT evaluation was performed or documented during this admission, which is clinically appropriate given the patient's functional profile. This assessment synthesizes nursing ADL documentation, physician mobility notes, and the patient's occupational history (bicycle courier) as a proxy for pre-morbid functional capacity. Confidence is moderate rather than high because I have not directly observed this patient ambulate, assessed gait quality at the bedside, or formally measured endurance. The level-of-care recommendation (no PT services indicated) is high-confidence based on the convergent picture of an otherwise healthy, high-functioning 42-year-old admitted for a metabolic event without functional sequelae. The fall risk assessment is moderate-confidence — the primary risk vector (hypoglycemia during courier work on an adjusted insulin regimen) is well-supported by the data, but formal fall risk scoring tools were not applied by PT.

physician.discharge_hold_reason: CRITICAL: Two teratogenic medications (lisinopril, atorvastatin) remain on active orders and must be discontinued and replaced (lisinopril) before patient leaves. Discharge is unsafe until these orders are corrected. Additional hard stops: no confirmed insulin access post-discharge in an uninsured patient with prior DKA x4 in 6 months; no MFM referral placed for a high-risk T1DM pregnancy at 7 weeks with HbA1c 11.4%.
physician.clinical_summary: Sarah Williams is a 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus since age 14 who presented in severe DKA (pH 7.14, bicarbonate 8, anion gap 28, Kussmaul respirations) and was found incidentally to be 7 weeks pregnant — a diagnosis unknown to her at admission. She was managed with IV insulin drip and fluids with complete DKA resolution by Day 3–4; current ABG and BMP are normal, glucose is 112 on a transitioned SQ regimen, and she is hemodynamically stable and tolerating oral intake. However, HbA1c of 11.4% identifies severely uncontrolled diabetes entering a high-risk first trimester, conferring substantial risk of fetal malformation, spontaneous abortion, and future DKA recurrence. Two home medications — lisinopril and atorvastatin — remain active in orders and are both absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. The patient is uninsured with no confirmed mechanism to obtain insulin after discharge, and no obstetric or MFM provider has been established. LACE score of 13 reflects highest-tier 30-day readmission risk, driven primarily by systemic SDOH barriers.

acute_issues_resolved.evidence: ABG Day 5: pH 7.39, bicarbonate 23, anion gap 12. Urine ketones trace. Glucose 112. Kussmaul respirations resolved. HR normalized from 128 to 80. Successfully transitioned to SQ insulin regimen tolerating diet.
Admission pH 7.14 corrected to 7.39; bicarbonate normalized from 8 to 23 mEq/L; anion gap closed from 28 to 12 mEq/L.
HR 80, RR 14 on current vitals. Admission HR 128, RR 26 with Kussmaul pattern.

Patient informed Day 2; social work consulted; prenatal vitamins and high-dose folic acid initiated. However, OB/MFM not yet established and prenatal ultrasound not yet completed — these remain active deficiencies.

HbA1c 11.4% on admission. Current glucose 112 reflects inpatient management only. Outpatient glycemic control remains critically inadequate for pregnancy; target <6.5%. This is a longitudinal issue requiring MFM and endocrinology, not a discharge-blocking acute finding per se, but context for urgency of referrals.

Recommend completing before discharge if feasible; if not, must be scheduled within 48 hours with confirmed appointment.

Not a lab — but administratively pending. Pregnancy qualifies for expedited Medicaid enrollment in most states. Must be actively pursued by social work before discharge. Directly determines whether insulin prescriptions are fillable.

medication_flags.detail: ACE inhibitor — absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy at all trimesters. Associated with fetal renal agenesis, oligohydramnios, renal tubular dysplasia, and neonatal death. Order remains ACTIVE as of case data. Patient is currently 7 weeks — discontinuation is urgent. Blood pressure must be managed with a pregnancy-safe agent.
medication_flags.action_required: DISCONTINUE IMMEDIATELY. Do not discharge with this medication. Replace with a pregnancy-safe antihypertensive — methyldopa, labetalol, or extended-release nifedipine are guideline-supported options (ACOG). Prescribing team to select agent and dose; do not discharge until replacement is ordered and patient is counseled.

medication_flags.detail: Statin — historically FDA Category X, contraindicated throughout pregnancy. Associated with fetal skeletal malformations and developmental toxicity in animal models. Order remains ACTIVE. Hyperlipidemia management should be deferred until postpartum.
medication_flags.action_required: DISCONTINUE IMMEDIATELY. Do not discharge with this medication active. No replacement needed during pregnancy — defer statin resumption to postpartum period. Document rationale in discharge summary.

Close outpatient titration required — endocrinology follow-up within 2 weeks is essential.

physician.confidence_note: Clinical data is comprehensive and internally consistent. DKA resolution is objective and well-documented. Medication contraindications are unambiguous per ACOG guidelines. The two critical safety flags (teratogenic medications) are supported by active order data explicitly documented in the case. Confidence would be limited only by the absence of imaging data (prenatal ultrasound not yet resulted) and uncertainty about real-time Medicaid enrollment status — neither of which changes the discharge hold determination.

Home urine ketone monitoring — when to check, what results mean, when to call vs. go to ED

Safe glucose targets in pregnancy (fasting <95 mg/dL, 1-hour post-meal <140 mg/dL, 2-hour post-meal <120 mg/dL per ADA pregnancy targets)

Insulin glargine dose increased from 18 to 22 units for pregnancy — patient must not revert to prior home dose from habit or assumption

Blood glucose <70 mg/dL that resolves with oral treatment (standard hypoglycemia) — report pattern to provider; do not assume it is acceptable in pregnancy
Urine ketones 1+ or greater on any morning check or when glucose exceeds 200 mg/dL
Systolic blood pressure >140 mmHg or diastolic >90 mmHg on two readings taken 15 minutes apart
Nausea or vomiting preventing oral intake for more than 12 hours (escalates DKA risk rapidly in pregnancy)
Unable to obtain insulin or afford medications — call before running out, not after
Any question about whether a medication is safe in pregnancy

Blood glucose >300 mg/dL regardless of ketone status
Blood glucose <54 mg/dL or any hypoglycemia with altered consciousness, inability to self-treat, or no improvement after 15 minutes of oral glucose
Vomiting and cannot keep any fluids or insulin down
Systolic BP >160 mmHg or diastolic >110 mmHg — hypertensive urgency in pregnancy
Severe headache, visual changes, right upper quadrant pain, or facial swelling (preeclampsia warning signs — even at 7 weeks, know the symptoms early)
Vaginal bleeding, cramping, or absence of expected pregnancy symptoms with concern for miscarriage
Chest pain, shortness of breath, or rapid heart rate at rest
Signs of severe DKA: deep/rapid breathing, fruity breath, extreme weakness, confusion, abdominal pain with high glucose

CRITICAL: If insulin is not secured before discharge, physical capacity to perform ADLs and work is at immediate risk — DKA can recur within 24–48 hours of insulin interruption in T1DM

Emotional state: patient received a pregnancy diagnosis 3 days ago in an ICU step-down unit while recovering from DKA. Psychological readiness to manage a suddenly intensified self-care regimen at home alone warrants explicit assessment before discharge
Studio apartment on month-to-month lease — no concern for physical access, but housing instability adds chronic stress that directly worsens glucose control

This is an ADL-level barrier — she cannot safely self-manage without this resolved.

flags.action: MFM (Maternal-Fetal Medicine) referral must be placed and appointment confirmed before discharge, target within 1 week. Standard OB is insufficient for this risk level. Care coordinator or MSW to make direct contact with MFM clinic, not just submit a referral form. Patient must leave with a confirmed appointment date, provider name, address, and phone number in hand.

Document patient's verbalized understanding in chart. flags.flag: HYPOGLYCEMIA RISK DURING BICYCLE COURIER WORK NOT ADDRESSED
flags.action: Patient's occupational activity (sustained aerobic cycling, irregular meals, variable schedule) combined with pregnancy insulin adjustments creates a significant hypoglycemia risk profile. Must educate before discharge: pre-shift glucose checks, minimum safe glucose to begin riding (recommend >120 mg/dL given pregnancy), mandatory fast-acting glucose on bicycle at all times (glucose tabs, gel), what to do if hypoglycemia occurs mid-route, and whether her insulin regimen needs further adjustment for exercise days. flags.flag: EMOTIONAL READINESS CONCERN — PREGNANCY DISCLOSED MID-HOSPITALIZATION IN CONTEXT OF ICU-LEVEL ILLNESS
flags.action: Patient was not aware of pregnancy at admission. Informed on Day 2 of a 5-day admission for DKA. Social work has been consulted but ongoing emotional status at time of discharge is not documented. Before final discharge teaching session, assess current psychological state. If patient is acutely overwhelmed, distressed, or not in a cognitive state to retain critical safety information, delay discharge teaching or provide written materials with a scheduled 24-hour follow-up call. Ensure social work has completed safety and coping assessment and that patient has mental health resources or hotline numbers on her discharge paperwork.

flags.flag: HbA1c 11.4% — FIRST TRIMESTER FETAL MALFORMATION RISK EDUCATION REQUIRED
flags.action: Patient must understand in non-alarming but clear terms that HbA1c of 11.4% in the first trimester is associated with increased risks of fetal malformation and requires urgent specialist involvement. This is the primary driver for why MFM (not standard OB) is the appropriate provider. Do not present this information in a way that induces panic, but patient must understand the urgency of glucose control and specialist follow-up — not as optional wellness goals but as fetal safety priorities.

flags.flag: FOOD INSECURITY WITH DIETARY CONSISTENCY REQUIREMENT FOR DM IN PREGNANCY
flags.action: Patient relies on food bank 2x/month. Consistent, low-glycemic nutrition is structurally required for glucose control in diabetic pregnancy but financially inaccessible. Refer to dietitian before discharge for practical, low-cost meal planning guidance compatible with current food access. Social work to identify WIC eligibility (she qualifies as a pregnant woman now), local food pantries with diabetic-appropriate options, and whether hospital social fund can provide a grocery bridge. This is not a compliance issue — it is a resource issue.

flags.flag: TRANSPORTATION BARRIER TO PRENATAL AND SPECIALIST APPOINTMENTS
flags.action: Patient has no car and uses bicycle for transportation. MFM and endocrinology follow-up appointments will be frequent (weekly to biweekly) for a high-risk diabetic pregnancy. Identify transportation assistance before discharge: Medicaid pending — once active, will likely include non-emergency medical transportation. In interim, explore community health worker escort programs, rideshare vouchers, or telehealth options for endocrinology. Document this barrier explicitly in care coordination notes so follow-up team is aware.

flags.flag: PRENATAL ULTRASOUND ORDERED BUT NOT COMPLETED
flags.action: Confirm whether ultrasound can be completed before discharge or arrange confirmed outpatient appointment within 48 hours. Patient must leave with specific date, time, and location. Dating ultrasound is important for establishing accurate gestational age and baseline fetal anatomy documentation given high HbA1c risk.

flags.flag: REPLACEMENT ANTIHYPERTENSIVE NOT YET PRESCRIBED
flags.action: Lisinopril must be discontinued and a pregnancy-safe antihypertensive ordered before discharge. As of chart review, no replacement has been prescribed. Physician must act on this today. Once prescribed, patient must receive education on the new medication: name, dose, timing, what to do if she misses a dose, and which BP readings require emergency care.

nurse.confidence_note: Clinical trajectory and lab data are well-documented and support the nursing assessment. Confidence is limited to 'moderate' for two reasons: (1) No teach-back documentation exists in the chart for any pregnancy-specific education topics, insulin regimen changes, or warning signs — education status across all domains must be treated as unconfirmed pending nursing assessment at bedside; (2) Patient's emotional state following unexpected pregnancy disclosure during acute DKA hospitalization is a significant wildcard for information retention and willingness to engage — chart documents social work consult on Day 2 but no current psychological readiness assessment at time of discharge planning. A bedside assessment of the patient's current emotional and cognitive readiness to receive and retain discharge education is the highest-priority nursing action before any formal discharge teaching begins.

Insulin aspart (sliding scale adjusted for pregnancy)
dose_changed: Insulin glargine — 18 units QHS (home) → 22 units QHS (discharge, adjusted for pregnancy insulin resistance)

Atorvastatin 20 mg daily — CRITICAL: still ACTIVE on discharge orders; must be discontinued before discharge (pregnancy contraindication)

drug_interactions.mechanism: Additive hypoglycemic effect from concurrent basal and prandial insulin — expected and intentional in basal-bolus regimen.
drug_interactions.clinical_implication: Hypoglycemia risk is elevated in pregnancy due to erratic meal timing, nausea/vomiting of early pregnancy, and variable activity levels (bicycle courier). First trimester hypoglycemia unawareness risk increases as pregnancy progresses.
drug_interactions.recommended_action: Ensure patient has glucagon emergency kit and understands hypoglycemia recognition and treatment, particularly in context of physical labor as courier. Reinforce meal-timing education and sick-day rules.

drug_interactions.mechanism: Prenatal vitamins typically contain 0.4–1.0 mg folic acid. Concurrent high-dose folic acid supplementation creates overlap.
drug_interactions.clinical_implication: Total folic acid intake may exceed 4 mg/day depending on prenatal vitamin formulation. Upper tolerable intake level for folic acid is generally considered 1 mg for the synthetic form in non-supplemented food, though high-dose supplementation in diabetic pregnancy is guideline-supported. Clinically, excess folic acid at these levels is not associated with toxicity, but should be documented.
drug_interactions.recommended_action: Verify folic acid content of specific prenatal vitamin formulation prescribed. If prenatal vitamin contains ≥0.4 mg folate, the 4 mg supplement should be prescribed with awareness of combined total. This combination is guideline-supported for T1DM pregnancy — confirm with MFM at first visit. No dose change anticipated but document intent.

dose_adjustment_flags.detail: Dose increased to 22 units QHS from home dose of 18 units QHS to account for pregnancy-related insulin resistance. HbA1c is 11.4% — target in pregnancy is <6.5%, representing a major management gap. Insulin requirements will increase substantially as pregnancy progresses (particularly second and third trimester). Current creatinine 0.8 mg/dL — renal function adequate, no dose adjustment needed on that basis. Pregnancy physiologically lowers serum creatinine; apparent 'normal' creatinine may underestimate true GFR changes.
dose_adjustment_flags.action_required: Close outpatient titration required — ideally by endocrinology or MFM-affiliated diabetes specialist. Dose will require upward adjustment over gestation. Flag for MFM to assume insulin management at first visit.

dose_adjustment_flags.detail: Sliding scale dosing adjusted for pregnancy. Fixed carbohydrate ratio would be preferable to sliding scale for tight gestational glycemic control, but requires patient education and outpatient support. Current sliding scale may be inadequate to achieve target HbA1c <6.5% needed in pregnancy.
dose_adjustment_flags.action_required: Endocrinology or MFM should transition to carbohydrate-to-insulin ratio dosing as soon as possible. Ensure patient has insulin-to-carb ratio prescribed or a plan to obtain one at first outpatient visit. Meal-timing education critical at discharge.

dose_adjustment_flags.detail: High-dose folic acid (4 mg) is guideline-recommended for women with pregestational diabetes to reduce neural tube defect risk — appropriate and intentional. Standard prenatal dose is 0.4–0.8 mg; the 4 mg dose is supported by ACOG for women with diabetes. Continue through at least end of first trimester, and MFM may extend through pregnancy.
dose_adjustment_flags.action_required: No adjustment needed. Confirm continuation plan with MFM at first visit.

contraindications.contraindication: ACE inhibitor use in pregnancy — absolute contraindication
contraindications.reason: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in all trimesters of pregnancy. Second and third trimester exposure is associated with fetal renal tubular dysplasia, renal agenesis, oligohydramnios, neonatal renal failure, skull hypoplasia, and death (FDA historical Category D/X designation for pregnancy). First trimester exposure carries risk of cardiovascular and CNS malformations per epidemiologic data, though the evidence is strongest for second/third trimester. At 7 weeks gestation, organogenesis is actively occurring. Order is currently ACTIVE on the discharge medication list.
contraindications.action_required: DISCONTINUE LISINOPRIL IMMEDIATELY — must not appear on discharge medication list. Physician must prescribe a pregnancy-safe antihypertensive (methyldopa, labetalol, or extended-release nifedipine are all ACOG-recommended first-line options for hypertension in pregnancy). BP at discharge 122/78 — physician should assess whether antihypertensive therapy is needed at all at current BP levels in pregnancy, or can be held with monitoring. This decision belongs to the prescriber, but the lisinopril order cannot leave with this patient.

contraindications.contraindication: Statin use in pregnancy — absolute contraindication
contraindications.reason: Statins are contraindicated in pregnancy. Atorvastatin was historically classified FDA Category X for pregnancy. Cholesterol and cholesterol-derived metabolites are essential for fetal development; HMG-CoA reductase inhibition disrupts these pathways. Animal and limited human data show fetal skeletal malformations and CNS defects. ACOG and ACC guidelines both recommend discontinuation immediately upon pregnancy recognition. Order is currently ACTIVE on the discharge medication list.
contraindications.action_required: DISCONTINUE ATORVASTATIN IMMEDIATELY — must not appear on discharge medication list. No replacement therapy needed during pregnancy; hyperlipidemia management is deferred to postpartum. Flag for primary care or cardiologist to reassess lipid management postpartum.

Irregular work schedule as bicycle courier disrupts meal timing and physical activity patterns, complicating glucose predictability
Food insecurity — inability to consistently access low-glycemic foods undermines dietary glycemic management
No established outpatient provider at discharge — gap in continuity of insulin titration and oversight
HbA1c 11.4% on admission suggests pre-admission adherence and/or access challenges were already present
4 ED visits for DKA in prior 6 months — pattern consistent with access-driven recurrence, not knowledge deficit
Positive factor: Has managed T1DM since age 14 — high disease familiarity and self-management knowledge base
Positive factor: Moderate-high health literacy, English-speaking, cognitively intact

access_affordability.detail: Insulin glargine (brand: Lantus, Basaglar, Toujeo) costs $289–$389 per vial without insurance. Patient requires 1–2 vials/month. DKA recurrence without insulin is near-certain within days.
solutions: Eli Lilly Insulin Value Program: insulin lispro (Humalog) available for $35/month cap — requires physician to switch glargine to lispro equivalent and patient to have Lilly product; confirm eligibility
Novo Nordisk Patient Assistance Program (NovoCare): may cover insulin degludec (Tresiba) or insulin detemir (Levemir) for uninsured patients — apply via manufacturer
Sanofi Insulins Valyou Savings Program: may provide Lantus at reduced cost for uninsured patients
Hospital charity care / social work: request 30-day discharge supply from inpatient pharmacy under charity care program before patient leaves

Expedite Medicaid enrollment: given confirmed pregnancy, patient may qualify for MassHealth (Massachusetts) Emergency Medicaid or accelerated pregnancy-related Medicaid — social work should pursue this immediately as it may activate within days

ReliOn NPH insulin at Walmart ($25/vial) as absolute last-resort bridge ONLY with comprehensive patient re-education on NPH pharmacokinetics — significant regimen change, requires physician order and pharmacist counseling session; not preferred given pregnancy complexity

access_affordability.detail: Insulin aspart (brand: NovoLog/NovoRapid) costs $285–$320 per vial without insurance. Patient requires 1–2 vials/month for prandial coverage. Same coverage gap as glargine.
solutions: Novo Nordisk NovoCare Patient Assistance Program: may cover NovoLog/insulin aspart for uninsured patients — social work to apply

Eli Lilly equivalent: insulin lispro (Humalog) available through Lilly Value Program at $35/month — requires physician to prescribe lispro instead of aspart (therapeutically interchangeable for most purposes, acceptable in pregnancy)
Hospital charity care: request 30-day discharge supply from inpatient pharmacy before discharge

ReliOn Regular insulin at Walmart as absolute last-resort bridge — significant pharmacokinetic difference from aspart; requires full re-education, physician order; not preferred in pregnancy due to hypoglycemia risk profile

access_affordability.detail: Prescription prenatal vitamins vary widely in cost. Patient is uninsured.
solutions: OTC prenatal vitamins (e.g., generic store brand) are clinically equivalent to prescription formulations for most patients and are significantly less expensive ($10–15/month)
Many WIC programs provide prenatal vitamins — enroll patient in WIC immediately given pregnancy and food insecurity
Community health center or OB clinic may provide samples at first visit

access_affordability.detail: High-dose folic acid 4 mg is a prescription formulation (OTC is 0.4–0.8 mg). Without insurance, this may require out-of-pocket cost.
solutions: GoodRx pricing for folic acid 1 mg tabs (taken as 4 tabs) or 5 mg tabs is typically under $5–10/month — very affordable even without insurance
Confirm pharmacy can dispense 1 mg tablets (4 tablets daily) as this is often more accessible and affordable than the single 4 mg tablet
Some community health centers and WIC programs dispense folic acid directly

access_affordability.detail: Once lisinopril is discontinued, a pregnancy-safe antihypertensive will need to be prescribed if BP monitoring indicates ongoing treatment need. Methyldopa and labetalol are generic and inexpensive; nifedipine ER is generic. These are low-cost medications even without insurance.
solutions: Methyldopa, labetalol, and nifedipine ER are all available as generic formulations for $4–15/month at most major pharmacies
GoodRx coupons can further reduce cost
No significant access barrier anticipated for the antihypertensive replacement itself — cost should not be a barrier

flags.flag: CRITICAL — TERATOGENIC MEDICATION ACTIVE: LISINOPRIL 10 MG STILL ON DISCHARGE ORDER LIST
flags.action: Physician must discontinue lisinopril before discharge order is finalized. This medication cannot be dispensed or sent home with a pregnant patient. Pharmacist should place a hard stop on dispensing lisinopril if order reaches pharmacy. A pregnancy-safe antihypertensive must be evaluated and prescribed if BP monitoring indicates ongoing need. This is a patient safety emergency.

flags.flag: CRITICAL — TERATOGENIC MEDICATION ACTIVE: ATORVASTATIN 20 MG STILL ON DISCHARGE ORDER LIST
flags.action: Physician must discontinue atorvastatin before discharge order is finalized. No replacement needed — hyperlipidemia management deferred to postpartum. Pharmacist should place a hard stop on dispensing atorvastatin if order reaches pharmacy.

flags.flag: CRITICAL — INSULIN AFFORDABILITY: UNINSURED PATIENT CANNOT AFFORD LIFE-SUSTAINING MEDICATIONS POST-DISCHARGE
flags.action: Social work and pharmacy must identify confirmed insulin supply (not just referral to a program) before patient is discharged. Options: hospital charity care 30-day supply, manufacturer assistance program enrollment with confirmed approval, or expedited Medicaid activation. Do not discharge without a verified plan for insulin in hand or confirmed to be at pharmacy within 24 hours. DKA recurrence without insulin in a pregnant T1DM patient is a dual life-threatening emergency.

flags.flag: HIGH — HbA1c 11.4% IN FIRST-TRIMESTER PREGNANCY: SEVERELY ELEVATED TERATOGENIC AND FETOTOXIC RISK
flags.action: MFM (Maternal-Fetal Medicine) specialist referral must be placed before discharge — standard OB is insufficient for this risk level. Tight glycemic management is urgent. Current insulin regimen requires close titration by subspecialist. Patient must understand the association between high blood glucose and fetal malformation risk — provide counseling without causing paralysis; frame as actionable and improvable.

flags.flag: HIGH — TSH 2.1 AT HIGH END OF PREGNANCY FIRST-TRIMESTER TARGET (0.1–2.5 mIU/L): THYROID MONITORING NEEDED
flags.action: TSH 2.1 is within the general reference range but is at the upper limit of the preferred first-trimester pregnancy range (0.1–2.5 mIU/L per ATA guidelines). Hypothyroidism in T1DM is common (autoimmune overlap). Recheck TSH at first MFM/OB visit. If TSH trends upward or patient develops symptoms, levothyroxine initiation will be needed. Flag for MFM to monitor — do not initiate treatment without recheck and clinical evaluation.

flags.flag: HIGH — COMPLEX INSULIN REGIMEN WITHOUT CONFIRMED OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP AT DISCHARGE
flags.action: Patient is being discharged on a basal-bolus insulin regimen with a dose that will require ongoing titration. Without confirmed endocrinology or MFM follow-up, insulin dose management falls to a PCP who has not yet been established. Ensure endocrinology appointment is placed within 2 weeks maximum. Consider diabetes care and education specialist (DCES) referral who can do remote insulin titration if in-person follow-up is delayed.

flags.flag: MODERATE — PRENATAL VITAMIN AND FOLIC ACID OVERLAP: CONFIRM COMBINED FOLATE DOSE
flags.action: Verify folic acid content of the specific prenatal vitamin formulation ordered. Document that combined folic acid intake is intentional and guideline-supported for diabetic pregnancy. Confirm with MFM at first visit.

flags.flag: MODERATE — HYPOGLYCEMIA RISK IN OCCUPATIONAL CONTEXT: BICYCLE COURIER WITH INTENSIFIED INSULIN REGIMEN IN PREGNANCY
flags.action: Patient's occupation as a bicycle courier involves unpredictable physical exertion, which significantly increases hypoglycemia risk on a basal-bolus insulin regimen. Patient must receive counseling on hypoglycemia prevention during work activity, must have rapid-acting glucose (glucose tablets, juice) accessible while cycling, and must have a glucagon emergency kit (or nasal glucagon) prescribed and filled at discharge. Advise patient on when it is unsafe to work due to glucose levels.

flags.flag: MODERATE — MEDICAID EXPEDITE: PREGNANCY SHOULD QUALIFY FOR ACCELERATED COVERAGE
flags.action: In Massachusetts, pregnant individuals typically qualify for MassHealth (Medicaid) with expedited enrollment. Social work should contact MassHealth directly to flag the active pregnancy and request emergency or expedited processing of the pending application. Coverage activation could dramatically reduce all downstream medication and prenatal care access barriers.

pharmacist.confidence_note: Confidence is high on all contraindication determinations (lisinopril and atorvastatin in pregnancy are absolute, well-established, guideline-supported contraindications), drug interaction assessments, and renal dose adequacy (creatinine 0.8 mg/dL with no hepatic concerns documented). Moderate uncertainty exists on the exact carbohydrate-to-insulin ratio and precise insulin titration needed — this requires subspecialty endocrinology or MFM input, not pharmacist determination. TSH flag is based on ATA pregnancy trimester-specific reference ranges which have some institutional variability — flagged for monitoring rather than intervention. Insulin program pricing reflects approximate market rates and may vary by geography and program eligibility at time of application.

housing.detail: Studio apartment, ground floor, solo occupant. Month-to-month lease with no security. Patient is reported to be 6 weeks behind on rent. Adding a high-risk pregnancy (with anticipated increased medical costs, potential work limitations, and newborn in approximately 32 weeks) to an already precarious housing situation materially elevates risk of housing loss. No lease protections in place. No partner or roommate to share costs.
housing.action: Connect to Boston Emergency Rental Assistance Program (ERAP) or Massachusetts RAFT program for rental arrears assistance — 6 weeks behind may be within eligible window before eviction proceedings. Identify housing case manager through community health center. Flag for housing social worker if available. Counsel patient on her rights under Massachusetts eviction moratorium protections and notice requirements. Begin conversation about housing planning for post-delivery — studio apartment with infant is viable short-term but crowding and cost will intensify.

food_security.detail: Patient relies on food bank 2x/month — this is active, documented food insecurity, not at-risk status. Dietary consistency is not optional for T1DM management; carbohydrate irregularity drives glucose instability and DKA risk. Gig income is irregular. Financial constraint limits ability to purchase low-glycemic foods, fresh produce, or diabetic-appropriate diet consistently. Pregnancy adds nutritional complexity (increased caloric and micronutrient needs). Food insecurity in this case is a direct DKA recurrence driver — the LACE E-score of 4 (4 prior ED visits) reflects this.
food_security.action: Enroll in WIC immediately — patient qualifies as pregnant with income below 185% FPL. WIC provides vouchers for specific food categories and diabetes-appropriate options. Connect to Greater Boston Food Bank or local pantry with diabetic-appropriate food options (low-sugar, high-protein). Refer for Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT) with diabetes-in-pregnancy dietitian — ideally through MFM clinic or community health center. Counsel patient that carbohydrate consistency is a clinical priority, not a preference, and connect this to insulin dosing education.

transportation.detail: No personal vehicle. Relies on bicycle for both work and transportation. Cannot reliably use bicycle to reach prenatal and specialist appointments due to weather, distance, and — importantly — hypoglycemia risk while cycling to/from medical appointments (fasting states, post-appointment fatigue). Public transit available but described as complex routing to OB/MFM clinic. Transportation barrier in a patient who requires weekly or biweekly MFM visits throughout a high-risk pregnancy represents a structural threat to care adherence that will directly increase readmission risk.
transportation.action: Enroll in MassHealth Transportation Benefit (Non-Emergency Medical Transportation, NEMT) — available upon Medicaid activation, provides free rides to medical appointments. As Medicaid bridge: connect to Boston EMS-affiliated community paramedicine programs or volunteer medical transport through local community health networks. Identify whether MFM clinic offers telehealth for some visits (reduces in-person transportation burden for lower-acuity check-ins). Provide patient with MBTA CharlieCard resources and route planning support. Flag that appointment scheduling should cluster visits on same day where clinically possible to minimize transportation trips.

medication_affordability.detail: UNINSURED. Requires insulin glargine (~$289–$389/vial) and insulin aspart (~$285–$320/vial) — combined monthly cost $500–$700 at retail. This is almost certainly unaffordable on irregular gig income. Without insulin, DKA recurrence is not a risk — it is a near-certainty within days of discharge. The prior 4 ED visits in 6 months strongly suggest this is not the first time insulin access has been interrupted. Additionally, newly required antihypertensive medication (to replace teratogenic lisinopril) adds to medication cost burden. Prenatal vitamins and high-dose folic acid add further out-of-pocket burden.
medication_affordability.action: MUST RESOLVE BEFORE DISCHARGE. Priority actions: (1) Confirm Eli Lilly Insulin Value Program enrollment — caps out-of-pocket at $35/month for Lilly insulins, no insurance required, available at participating retail pharmacies; provide patient with program card before discharge. (2) Hospital charity care authorization for 30-day supply as backup if manufacturer program has processing delay. (3) Novo Nordisk Patient Assistance Program as secondary option. (4) Medicaid expedited enrollment (see insurance actions) — if activated, eliminates this barrier entirely. (5) If all else fails and patient is counseled appropriately: ReliOn NPH/Regular at Walmart ($25) is a last-resort option — but requires full re-education on timing and pharmacokinetics and carries higher hypoglycemia risk; this should be a safety net only, documented carefully. Do NOT discharge without confirmed insulin in hand.

social_isolation.detail: Lives alone. Separated from partner 3 months ago (same timeframe as insurance loss — these events are concurrent stressors). Primary support is mother Diane Williams, 2 hours away with her own health limitations. No partner support. Gig work (solo bicycle courier) is inherently isolating. Patient received news of unexpected pregnancy on hospital Day 2 after an ICU-level admission — this is a significant acute psychosocial stressor layered on top of chronic SDOH burden. Emotional support network is thin. Patient expressed 'shock and distress' on pregnancy disclosure. Risk of antenatal depression and anxiety is elevated. Social isolation is not yet at 'isolated' level given mother's presence and patient's baseline functioning, but trajectory is concerning particularly as pregnancy advances.
social_isolation.action: Conduct brief psychosocial screening for depression and anxiety before discharge (Edinburgh Prenatal Depression Scale or PHQ-9 appropriate). Connect to peer support program for pregnant women with diabetes (JDRF T1D pregnancy communities, local diabetes peer navigator programs). Explore whether Fenway Health or South End CHC offers integrated behavioral health co-located with prenatal care. Counsel on importance of support network and assist patient in identifying even one additional support person. Follow up on emotional processing of unexpected pregnancy — patient may benefit from short-term counseling, which can be arranged through community health center.

safety_concerns.concern: Hypoglycemia while working as solo bicycle courier — patient works alone, on a bicycle, in traffic, with significantly adjusted insulin regimen and pregnancy altering glucose dynamics. A hypoglycemic episode while cycling is a fall and traffic safety risk.
safety_concerns.action: Ensure diabetes educator or clinical team addresses hypoglycemia safety during courier work before discharge — patient should carry rapid-acting glucose (glucose tabs, juice) at all times while working, know when to stop cycling and treat, and have a glucagon kit accessible. MSW role: confirm patient has access to affordable glucose monitoring supplies and rapid glucose sources (these are also cost-affected by uninsured status). Flag for team that work safety counseling is needed.

safety_concerns.concern: Uninsured status creating rationing risk — previous LACE E-score of 4 (four ED visits in 6 months) is consistent with patients who ration insulin due to cost, leading to recurrent DKA. If bridge program fails or patient cannot fill prescription, she will ration. This is a predictable, preventable safety event.
safety_concerns.action: Insulin must be physically in patient's hands before discharge, not just a referral given. Confirm fill, not just referral. Document in discharge note that patient was provided a filled 30-day supply or confirmed program enrollment with immediate access. No exceptions.

safety_concerns.concern: Antenatal psychological distress — patient received unexpected pregnancy news in the ICU setting after a severe DKA admission. She expressed shock and distress. Concurrent stressors include separation from partner, insurance loss, housing instability, and food insecurity. Unaddressed psychological distress in high-risk pregnancy is associated with poor glucose control, missed appointments, and adverse perinatal outcomes.
safety_concerns.action: Administer validated prenatal depression screen (EPDS) before discharge. If elevated, connect to integrated behavioral health through community health center or crisis line if needed. Provide warm handoff, not just a list of resources. Normalize that her response to this news is understandable and that support is available.

safety_concerns.concern: Domestic safety — partner separation 3 months ago is recent and concurrent with multiple major life stressors. No documentation of domestic violence screening found in available case data.
safety_concerns.action: Conduct brief IPV/domestic safety screen before discharge using validated tool (HITS or WAST). Document result. If concerns identified, connect to SafeLink Massachusetts (1-877-785-2020) and ensure safety planning before discharge. Note: gig work as sole income source post-separation may reflect financial control dynamics — screen thoughtfully.

Provide patient with Boston Housing Authority pregnancy preference information (pregnant women receive preference points on public housing waitlists in MA — this process should start now given 12+ month waitlists). Connect to housing case manager through community health center.

flags.flag: CRITICAL BARRIER — MEDICATION AFFORDABILITY: Patient is uninsured with $500–$700/month insulin cost. Prior 4 DKA ED visits in 6 months are consistent with insulin rationing. Discharge without confirmed insulin access is predictably unsafe and will result in readmission within days.
flags.action: Insulin must be physically confirmed (filled prescription or confirmed same-day pharmacy access) before patient exits the building. Eli Lilly Insulin Value Program and hospital charity care are primary mechanisms. This is a non-negotiable discharge condition.

flags.action: Escalate to attending physician and pharmacist TODAY. MSW role is to flag and document escalation — clinical team must action the discontinuation and replacement. MSW to confirm resolved before completing discharge checklist.

flags.flag: HIGH — NO MFM OR OB ESTABLISHED: 7-week pregnancy with T1DM and HbA1c 11.4% requires urgent MFM involvement. Standard OB alone is insufficient for this risk level. No prenatal care of any kind established.

flags.flag: HIGH — TRANSPORTATION BARRIER TO FREQUENT HIGH-RISK PRENATAL APPOINTMENTS: Patient relies on bicycle, no vehicle, complex public transit route to OB/MFM. A patient who requires weekly or biweekly specialist visits and cannot reliably get there will accrue missed appointments rapidly.
flags.action: NEMT enrollment upon Medicaid activation is priority. Identify interim transportation bridge. Explore telehealth availability for some MFM visits. Schedule appointments to cluster on same day where possible.

flags.flag: HIGH — FOOD INSECURITY AS DIRECT DKA RISK FACTOR: Active food insecurity (food bank 2x/month) combined with inability to afford low-glycemic foods is a mechanistic contributor to glucose instability and DKA recurrence. This is not a background social factor — it is a clinical risk driver.
flags.action: WIC enrollment before or immediately after discharge. Connect to food pantry with diabetic-appropriate options. Medical nutrition therapy referral. Frame food security intervention explicitly in clinical terms in the discharge plan.

flags.flag: MEDIUM — DOMESTIC SAFETY NOT DOCUMENTED: No IPV/domestic safety screening found in available case data. Recent partner separation (3 months ago) with concurrent financial control indicators (lost insurance through partner) warrants assessment.
flags.action: Complete IPV screen before discharge. Document result. Provide SafeLink Massachusetts information regardless of screening result as standard practice.
